title: The First Step in Standardizing an Artificial Aging Protocol for Dental Composites—Evaluation of Basic Protocols
author: Szczesio-Wlodarczyk, Agata
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This research was funded by the National Science Centre, Poland [grant number: UMO-2020/37/N/ST5/00191, 2021]. 

 The publication is a summary of the first step of the project, that aims standardizing an artificial aging protocol for dental composites. So far, no attempt has been made to design a standard protocol to estimate dental composite clinical performance in a practical and effective way. Dental materials do not have to undergo research to validate their performance over their predicted lifetime upon introduction to the market. A standardized aging procedure is crucial for improving the resistance of dental resin composite to oral conditions. Research on this topic received funding from the Preludium grant from the National Science Center, Poland. 

 In this paper three materials were tested to compare the degradation of resin from that of the filler and resin–filler interface. The first material (Resin F) was unfilled resin. The second and third materials were composites based on a similar resin matrix to Resin F but with differentiated fillers content. The influence of six aging protocol on the material properties of the samples was determined based on flexural strength (FS), diametral tensile strength (DTS), Vickers hardness (HV) and microstructure evaluation. To overall results indicate that the resin matrix and is more susceptible to degradation processes. The most aggressive aging protocol used 0.1M NaOH during seven days at 60°C. The need for accurate selection of the thermal and chemical factors acting during artificial aging was emphasized. 

 On the basis of the obtained data, three protocols were selected for the second part of the project (standardization of artificial aging protocol for dental composites). In this stage, it is planned to supplement the basic tests (FS, DTS, HV) for selected protocols (Protocol 5, Protocol 4+5; Protocol 6+5), with fatigue evaluation and fracture toughness test. A wider and more accurate selection of factors and research methods during artificial aging protocols will provide a clearer picture of the prolonged clinical performance of dental composites. 

 Authors hope to determine an artificial aging method for evaluating the clinical performance of dental composites. Our publication is first attempt to determine standard aging protocol for dental composites.
